text:
An API, or Application Programming Interface, allows different software applications to communicate with each other. When working with APIs, you primarily use HTTP requests to send and receive data. The most common methods are GET, to retrieve information, and POST, to create new data.

Data is often returned in JSON format, which is lightweight and easy for both humans and machines to read. Many APIs require authentication, usually through an API key, to ensure that only authorised users can access the system. It's also important to understand rate limits, which restrict how many requests you can make in a given time period.

Finally, always implement error handling. This helps your application respond gracefully when an API is unavailable or returns unexpected data, improving the reliability of your code.
